sentences.text: My name is Emil Jacoby.
E-M-I-L J-A-C-O-B-Y.
I was born Jacobovitz, J-A-C-O-B-O-V-I-T-Z.
I was born on October the 10th, 1923.
I was born in a village named Terebla, T-E-R-E-B-L-A, Terebla, in an area called, at that time, Carpatoros, which belonged to Czechoslovakia.
My father's name-- he was born Mendel Jacobovitz.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] His father's name was [?
Lieb ?] Jacobovitz, and his mother was [?
Chendel ?].
They lived in a very, very small village, [?
Chandrov ?], not far from Bushtina, where we lived after my father married my mother.
They moved from Terebla to Bushtina.
Bushtina's a bigger city, a bigger town, than all  those small villages.
My father, as a young boy, he [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] emigrated to the United States to help his family.
And he stayed in the United States as a naturalized citizen, American citizen.
He stayed in Chicago, Illinois for almost 20 years.
I don't remember the exact years, but between 15 and 20 years he was there. 17, maybe 18, years.
He came back after World War I to visit his hometown.
And he married-- it was before World War I-- to visit his hometown.
And he married a girl from Terebla by the name of Leia.
And he went back to the United States to take her out, to send her the papers.
In the meantime, war broke out-- World War I broke out-- and he couldn't get her home to take her out.
So he was waiting until the war was over.
By the time the war got over, World War I, his wife bore a girl, a daughter for him, and his wife passed away.
It was some kind of an illness.
I don't know what was the illness she was suffering.
And she passed away.
So after World War I, he returned to his hometown to see his daughter.
And he married my first wife-- his first wife's sister, which was my mother.
And she wouldn't go with him back to America.
She kept him there in Terebla.
And he remained there-- he married her and he went over to Bushtina, which was a bigger town, to settle down.
He brought home a lot of dollars, which made him a very well-to-do person in that time.
So he built his own house, his own business-- a wholesale business-- and he was doing very well for a while.
So I was born.
When he came to Bushtina, I was already born.
I was a small boy.
And I remember we had a big business and I had nannies.
We had maids and helpers in the business.
And I was very spoiled kid when I was a little boy.
But then later, when I grew up and I start go to school, business went down.
It was a wholesale business selling wheat for the small stores in the area.
He came to buy big bags of wheat and flour.
Then he had, also, a general store.
And also, he was having a farm, which people working with cows and horses.
So when he went bankrupt because people couldn't pay up the debts for buying on credit, so just gave up the wholesale, other big business, and he remained with this general store.
So I used to help out, also, in the store for my father.
And after I finished the elementary [?
Czech ?] school and junior high, I was also-- my father signed me up to high school, which took me almost an hour by train to get there.
So one day-- Tell me where the school was.
The school was in the city by the name of Chust, C-H-U-S-T-- Chust.
It was a large city.
And I went there.
I started in September, 1st of September, to go to school, but I'm not sure I made two months in the school.
I had to go there Friday evening, to Chust, because I had to go to high school on Saturday, too, on the sabbath, in order not to violate sabbath.
And I was raised in an orthodox religious family.
I had to go there and sleep over in Chust in my aunt's house.
Aunt's name was Miriam.
Last name was [?
Leibowitz ?].
I'm not sure it's [?
Leibowitz ?].
That's what I think, it [? was Leibowitz. ?]
So what I remember is one Friday night, I wake up in the middle of the night with a big noise of Czech soldiers marching towards a military camp located not far from my aunt.
And I was wondering what is going on?
Such a [INAUDIBLE], such a [INAUDIBLE].

So in the morning, I went to school, and I found on the main entrance of the school a sign-- handwritten sign-- the school, the class, will be closed until further note due to the general mobilization of the military forces, which will be staged at the school.
So this was my last day in school-- the mobilization of Czechoslovakia due to the crisis, which took place in 1938.
When Hitler met Chamberlain, the prime minister of Britain, and Deladier, prime minister of France, they agreed to sacrifice part of Czechoslovakia in order for Hitler not to invade Czechoslovakia.
That was the crises of that time which broke out, and Jewish communities were very concerned at that time that Hitler is going to invade Czechoslovakia.
And Jews were, I remember, in the synagogue.
Their concern was noticed during the prayers in the synagogue when they were crying loud out during to prayer on sabbath-- oh, God, help us to overcome these difficult times.
They were very concerned.
But then, after a few sermons, which the rabbi-- which his name was [INAUDIBLE]-- came, and also the rabbi from [INAUDIBLE].
And big rabbis used come to our town and held sermons.
And they would touch this crisis during the sermon, giving them hope, telling them not to fear this terrible thing which is going on.

Because first of all, he tried to make people to relax and to calm down, telling them, like, God always came to help of the Jewish people, not to let them get destroyed, like Haman during Purim, making miracles all the time in history.
So they would not fear.
But he would always mention that we have to fear more a danger not from Hitler, he says.
Hitler is a politician, and he has to make this noise about anti-Semitism because he wants to-- actually, what he wants to do is not kill Jews, the rabbi would say.
His main purpose and objective is to fight the Soviet Russia, which was creating a big danger not only for Germany, but for the whole world.

And at that time, he convinced even the superpowers like America and Britain and France to help Hitler to get arms and financial help to get strong and be able to stand against the danger of the-- he called it Judeo-Bolshevik danger, which was coming from there, from the east.
So the rabbi said, his purpose is actually to fight communism.
And we Jews, we should be neutral, at least, because what can we lose if these two two large powers, Germany and Russia, will fight each other?
So these two nations can kill each other, and we should stay neutral, not to be on this side against Germany or with Russia.
Because we don't have nothing.
We might lose.
Russia is actually against the Jews because they're atheist, against God.
And many Jews who believe in Russia, they became also atheist.
And they start to be assimilated.
So we are losing Jews, actually, Jews from our nation, our people, without the war.
Only because they became assimilated Jews.
This is also a type of a holocaust, he says.
He tried to calm us down and explain to us we should not be alarmed because of the situation, of the whole situation.
So the other thing was, that he was trying to explain to us, the rabbi-- I told you, I said the assimilation thing.

So when we got home with my father, on my way home-- it took us about half an hour to get home from the synagogue to our home-- I was with him to hear this sermon of the rabbi, and I already at that time knew from our teacher in high school, I mean in the junior high, that Hitler wants to invade Czechoslovakia.
And the teacher told us that Hitler is putting people, Jewish people or people who disagree with his policy, into concentration camps.
He showed us a picture about Dachau.
For the first time in my life I heard the name Dachau.
I didn't know there are concentration camps.
And he explained us exactly what a concentration camp means.
And then he was also telling us about the book Hitler wrote, the book which he called, 'Mein kampf' in German means 'my fight.' My fight.
So he told us that these things-- when I asked my father, listen, what is the rabbi telling you, actually?
I don't understand.
Give me some explanation.
Was he telling you that we should be more fearful of Stalin than of Hitler?
He says, exactly.
That's what he said.
I said, why?
Because listen, he says-- my father-- the rabbi is-- first of all, when he's talking, he's talking-- you know, he is a rabbi.
He is our closest spiritual leader, which we have to believe.
We have a choice, my father says, to believe in the politician, which they write in the papers and the radios one thing, or to believe in the synagogue when it comes to rabbis.
Our rabbi, our spiritual leader.
So that is the way.
We believe more what he is saying.
So we're supposed to believe what he is saying, but we have to keep our mind open and see if [INAUDIBLE] like it is.
I says, listen, my dad, my teacher is telling me something else.
And I'm very worried that my rabbi is not telling me the whole truth.
Because our teacher's telling me what the book says, what he's writing in his book, that he wants to destroy-- he wants to eliminate all the Jews.
Yeah, he says, but listen, don't take it too seriously.
He might be a politician.
He might also, like the rabbi says, he might only do it for political purposes, and he doesn't mean it.
So I was like-- you know, I was amazed.
And I said, Dad, maybe we should think about it seriously.
And you are American citizen.
And we have now still a chance to go away to America, back to America.
You don't have business here.
The business is slow.
My wife says, no way.
He says, I'm not going to go back.
Now the house, the real estate is very low.
And if we go to American now, with my age, what am I going to do there?
I don't have-- I used to go there as a young man, I used to work hard, I made money.
Now if I go to America, there is a recession over there, I read in the paper.
Unemployment.
What am I going to do?
I don't have the money to go and invest in money.
What is going to happen with all the Jews like the rabbi says?
That's what the rabbi said.
What has happened with [INAUDIBLE] Israel will happen with us, too.
We have [? to ?] trust in God.
We believe he will save us.
He will protect us.
So we have to stay.
Well, and then I remember another episode.
Once we had a tenant in our house, and she was a German.
She was a business woman.
She spoke German and a little Czech.
And she was dealing with-- we had a lot of wooded areas.
They cut out all this, the trees, and [INAUDIBLE] delivering it on water through Czechoslovakia.
There was a big river, the Tisa and the [INAUDIBLE]-- you heard about these big rivers?
And she was the businessman who were coming and buying and dealing.
And she was living in our house and paying rent.
And sometimes we would invite her for a dinner on a Friday, on a sabbath evening.
Very nice lady.
So at that time, when it took place in Munich, this crisis-- I don't know why this come to talk about.
She was telling, don't worry, Mr.
Jacobovitz.
Hitler is not such a bad man, she said.
And I said, yeah, but he has wrote the [INAUDIBLE] My father says, you shut up.
Don't talk like this.
Her name was Mrs.
Zellinger ?].
Mrs.
Zellinger ?] Let Mrs.
Zellinger ?] speak.
And Mrs.
Zellinger ?] says, let him speak, let him speak.
He was probably brainwashed in school by some communist in school, socialist.
He doesn't know what he's talking about.
Hitler doesn't want to kill all the Jews.
He is anti-Semitic like any other.
We have anti-Semites all over the world.
He is.
He may be anti-Semite, but he is now a politician, so he cannot afford to eliminate all the Jews.
The Jews bring business.
The Jews are all over the world.
He has good relations with America, with Britain, with France.
He takes money from them.
He needs to buy stuff from them.
So he cannot afford to eliminate all the Jews.

What he's going to do, she said, he might be willing to put in concentration camps those Jews who are communist or were doing some anti-Hitler, some propaganda against Hitler, or who are swindlers, who are doing something-- crooks, like this.
But honest people like Mr.
Jacobovitz, religious people believing in God, she said, these people Hitler is not going to touch.
So I couldn't say anything yet.
And then my father looked at me and says, you don't talk like this when Mrs.
Zellinger ?] is talking.
So that was the idea at that time, right?
That on one hand, we believe the rabbi.
That he was, like, our spiritual leader, the closest to God.
He was-- when something had a problem, we turned always to our rabbi.
Not only-- in anything, which want to get some help.
Even to a person who was sick-- you didn't go first to a doctor in our town, you went to a rabbi.
So of course we believed him.
He said something, we believed in it.
And that was the problem that Jews were not enough alert when it was still time to do something.
They underestimated this danger of Hitler, which was around the corner.
And he overestimated the danger of assimilation.
[INAUDIBLE] if a Jew will  marry a non-Jewish girl or he will become a secular Jew and then go to Palestine-- at that time, Palestine was a no-no because no Jew was allowed to go to Palestine.
That is only a messiah will come-- something like that.
So this thing confused-- or they kept the Jews under such a-- it was a climate.
They created a climate.

In this climate, these words which these rabbis were, at that time, uttering created a climate that the Jews were so disarmed, so blind, so believing in these words, when it came already to a situation that they wanted to do something, it was already too late.
I want to take you back a little bit to your childhood.
Tell me where you lived at the time, the address.
At that time, I was living-- we were living on the street close to the train station.
They call it already in Czech.
The train station is [? nadrazi ?] and the street is [?
Ulice. ?] 158 [? nadrazi ?] [?
Ulice. ?] that was our address.
Bushtina.
And you mentioned your father, but you didn't say your mother.
So tell me your mother's name. 1 My mother's name was Rachel in Jewish-- [?
Rkhl ?] in Jewish.
And in Hebrew, it's Rachel.
And in non-Jewish, she was Rose.
Her name was Rose in the papers.
And her maiden name?
Her maiden name was Pickel, P-I-C-K-E-L-- Pickel.
No.
My mother was-- she was housewife all the time.
And sometimes she would step in, and when my father had to go somewhere out of our business with a train or to another, to take care of some business outside.
So she would come and give a hand.
She was very efficient in business, even more than my father.
She was very nice and relaxed with people.
My father was more a strict man.
He didn't have this business talent like my mother.
He would be more [INAUDIBLE].
He was a hard worker.
He used to work very hard in America.
Her name is Hannah.
Yes, two brothers.
My middle brother name was, in Hebrew, [?
Bezalel. ?] And my father called him Charlie.
And that's how he was listed in school and the papers, Charlie.
Charlie Jacobovitz.
And then the smaller brother was Jewish name [INAUDIBLE].
And when I left home, he didn't go to school yet, so I don't know what in the papers-- what name they gave him in the papers.
I don't--
Well, the cheder-- I started in a early age, about 5.
When I was 5, already I went to Jewish school.
It was one room, one large room, with about 20 boys around a big, large table, sitting.
And in head of the table was sitting the rabbi with the beard and with the payots and with the yarmulke, and with a stick in his hand.
And he wouldn't tolerate if somebody would ask too much questions.
And I was not-- most of the time, I would raise my hand-- I have a question.
He would smack me over my fingers.
No questions now.
So I was the age of 5, and I was there till the age of 13.
Many years I've studied yeshiva.
I studied the Talmud, the Bible.
I studied how to pray in the synagogue from the Torah, which is not everybody knows how to do it.
And many things I studied, but now already-- nowadays, I don't remember too much.
And the Czech school was a Czech school over there.
It was a large school in Bushtina.
I went five years.
I studied in this elementary school for five years.
After five years, I went to junior high in another place, another town.
They called it [?
Tiachovo. ?]
